summary: Jeff Atwood examines freelance coding marketplaces like Elance, oDesk, and RentACoder with skepticism about their practical value. He cites Dan Appleman's analysis showing these platforms are plagued by unrealistic projects, global wage competition, and high failure rates. While acknowledging his own bias as a professional programmer, Atwood finds the challenges of remote specification writing and project management make these sites impractical. He concludes that while these marketplaces shouldn't be dismissed entirely, professional developers should be able to demonstrate more value than anonymous low-bid contractors.
key_points: Freelance coding marketplaces create global wage competition where developers in low-cost regions can underbid significantly
Many posted projects are unrealistic (like eBay clones for $500), leading to frequent failures and rip-offs on both sides
Writing specifications is difficult enough with coworkers in the same room—doing it across thousands of miles with strangers compounds the challenge
These sites serve as a useful baseline for professional developers to demonstrate their superior value to employers
The author compares these platforms to dating sites—potentially useful, but a sign of trouble if someone can't find stable client relationships
money_quotes: As professional programmers, I think we're all naturally inclined to see these sort of low-bid contract sites as cannibalizing and cheapening our craft.

You'll be amazed at how little people in some parts of the world will bid. That's because a few dollars an hour can work well in a country where the average wage is a couple of hundred dollars a month.
What ends up happening in these cases is that usually somebody ends up getting ripped off (either the client or the consultant who underbid or fails to deliver).
Specification documents are hard enough to write when everyone involved is a coworker sitting in the same room.
They might be one way to find a client relationship, but I'd be highly suspicious of any professional developer who can't find a stable, long term relationship with a client eventually.
Surely you can demonstrate better value to your employer than the random, anonymous programmers on Elance, guru.com, RentACoder, or oDesk.
key_insight: Low-bid freelance coding marketplaces are plagued by unrealistic expectations and communication challenges, but professional developers should view them as a baseline against which to demonstrate their superior value.
